To find the determinant of the given $4 \times 4$ matrix, let's denote the matrix as $A$:
$$
A = \left(\begin{array}{cccc}
 \frac{4}{3} & 1 & -\frac{9}{2} & -\frac{13}{3} \\
 -\frac{13}{3} & \frac{25}{6} & -\frac{19}{6} & \frac{11}{6} \\
 -\frac{23}{6} & \frac{29}{6} & -\frac{10}{3} & -\frac{19}{6} \\
 -\frac{17}{6} & -\frac{7}{6} & \frac{5}{2} & \frac{7}{3}
\end{array}\right)
$$

### Step 1: Simplify the matrix by eliminating denominators
To make the calculations easier, we can factor out common denominators from the rows or columns.
Notice the denominators are $2$ and $3$. The least common multiple is $6$.
Let's rewrite each element with a denominator of $6$:
$$
A = \left(\begin{array}{cccc}
 \frac{8}{6} & \frac{6}{6} & -\frac{27}{6} & -\frac{26}{6} \\
 -\frac{26}{6} & \frac{25}{6} & -\frac{19}{6} & \frac{11}{6} \\
 -\frac{23}{6} & \frac{29}{6} & -\frac{20}{6} & -\frac{19}{6} \\
 -\frac{17}{6} & -\frac{7}{6} & \frac{15}{6} & \frac{14}{6}
\end{array}\right)
$$
We can factor out $\frac{1}{6}$ from each of the 4 rows. Since the determinant is linear in each row, factoring out $\frac{1}{6}$ from all 4 rows results in a factor of $(\frac{1}{6})^4 = \frac{1}{1296}$.
$$
